coredata.dc:title: Construction of mitochondria targeted and FRET based ratiometric sensing nanoplatform for sulfur dioxide accurate detection in vitro and in vivo
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Wen
Li, Jinyao
Wang, Zicheng
Tian, Yafei
Ren, Guodong
Hou, Xiaoyu
Guo, Lixia
Li, Lihong
Zhang, Chengwu
Wu, Zhifang
Yan, Lili
Li, Sijin
Diao, Haipeng
coredata.dc:description: Sulfur dioxide (SO2) is a key molecule in organisms that is involved in the regulation of different physiological procedures. Aberrant SO2 causes a variety of diseases, such as cancer and neurodegeneration. Thus, sensitive and selective detection of SO2 is of great importance. Based on the Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) between green fluorescence carbon dots (GCDs) donor and amide-linked near-infrared fluorescence emissive organic small molecular dye (CDDBT) acceptor, one ratiometric fluorescent nano platform, Mito-GCDs-CDDBT for mitochondria SO2 sensing was constructed. In this FRET sensing system, CDDBT served as the receptor for SO2, and the presence of SO2 enhanced GCDs green fluorescence signal and quenched CDDBT near-infrared fluorescence signal due to the disruption of FRET. Mito-GCDs-CDDBT could sensitively detect SO2 with a detection limit of as low as 0.701 μM. Meanwhile, Mito-GCDs-CDDBT achieved fluorescence imaging to measure the response of cellular exogenous and endogenous SO2 with remarkable mitochondrial targeting. Moreover, Mito-GCDs-CDDBT also realized SO2 sensing in vivo including zebrafish and mice. The as-prepared versatile nanoplatform displayed several advantages, such as mitochondria targeting, FRET-based sensitive detection, and sensing capabilities in biological milieu. Potentially, it could be applied in the diagnostics of SO2 involved diseases.
coredata.dcterms:subject: FRET nano platform
Mitochondria targeted
SO2 accurate detection
Bioimaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Selective response of Mito-GCDs-CDDBT to SO2
Fluorescence imaging of Mito-GCDs-CDDBT in vitro and in vivo
Design of mitochondria-targeted and ratiometric sensing platform for SO2
Fabrication, characterization and optical properties of GCDs
Preparation and characterization of FRET nano-probe (Mito-GCDs-CDDBT)

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) XPS spectrum of GCDs, (B) TEM micrograph of GCDs (scale bar is 20 nm), (C) TEM diameter distribution of GCDs; (D) AFM image of GCDs; (E) AFM height profile of GCDs; (F) FT-IR spectrum of GCDs; (G) XRD pattern of GCDs; (H) The UV–Visible absorption spectrum, best excitation spectrum and best emission spectrum of GCDs; (I) Fluorescence emission spectra of GCDs at different excitation wavelengths.

(A) TEM micrograph of Mito-GCDs-CDDBT (scale bar is 10 nm, inset: TEM diameter distribution of GCDs-CDDBT); (B) Zeta potential of GCDs and Mito-GCDs-CDDBT; (C) The normalized absorption and fluorescence emission spectra (Ex = 420 nm for GCDs; Ex = 500 nm for CDDBT) of GCDs and CDDBT; (D) The absorption and fluorescence emission spectra of Mito-GCDs-CDDBT (Ex = 420 nm).

(A) Histograms of F560/F690 in the presence of ions including Na+, K+, Cl-, F-, Br-, I-,

HSO

HSO
3
-
(100 μM), scale bar: 300 µm; (B) Histograms of relative quantified fluorescent intensities of Mito-GCDs-CDDBT in different organ tissue sections at green channel and red channel separately.

HSO
3
-
(20 μM and 100 μM), (Scale bar: 200 µm).

In vivo fluorescence images of BALB/c nude mice of Mito-GCDs-CDDBT (0.120 mg/mL) in the presence and absence of
HSO
3
-
(100 μM) by subcutaneous injection (left) and intratumoral injection (right).
Schematic illustration for mitochondria-targeted FRET-based ratiometric probe for sensing of SO2 in vitro and in vivo.

head.ce:title: Construction of mitochondria targeted and FRET based ratiometric sensing nanoplatform for sulfur dioxide accurate detection in vitro and in vivo

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Nuclear Medicine, The First Hospital of Shanxi Medical University, Taiyuan 030001, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: The First Hospital of Shanxi Medical University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Nuclear Medicine, The First Hospital of Shanxi Medical University, Taiyuan 030001, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Basic Medical Sciences, Shanxi Medical University, Taiyuan 030001, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Basic Medical Sciences, Shanxi Medical University, Taiyuan 030001, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Cellular Physiology, Ministry of Education, Shanxi Medical University, Taiyuan 030001, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Cellular Physiology, Ministry of Education, Shanxi Medical University, Taiyuan 030001, PR China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: College of Basic Medical Sciences, Shanxi Medical University, Taiyuan 030001, PR China (W. Liu and H. Diao).

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Sulfur dioxide (SO2) is a key molecule in organisms that is involved in the regulation of different physiological procedures. Aberrant SO2 causes a variety of diseases, such as cancer and neurodegeneration. Thus, sensitive and selective detection of SO2 is of great importance. Based on the Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) between green fluorescence carbon dots (GCDs) donor and amide-linked near-infrared fluorescence emissive organic small molecular dye (CDDBT) acceptor, one ratiometric fluorescent nano platform, Mito-GCDs-CDDBT for mitochondria SO2 sensing was constructed. In this FRET sensing system, CDDBT served as the receptor for SO2, and the presence of SO2 enhanced GCDs green fluorescence signal and quenched CDDBT near-infrared fluorescence signal due to the disruption of FRET. Mito-GCDs-CDDBT could sensitively detect SO2 with a detection limit of as low as 0.701 μM. Meanwhile, Mito-GCDs-CDDBT achieved fluorescence imaging to measure the response of cellular exogenous and endogenous SO2 with remarkable mitochondrial targeting. Moreover, Mito-GCDs-CDDBT also realized SO2 sensing in vivo including zebrafish and mice. The as-prepared versatile nanoplatform displayed several advantages, such as mitochondria targeting, FRET-based sensitive detection, and sensing capabilities in biological milieu. Potentially, it could be applied in the diagnostics of SO2 involved diseases.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) have been developed as a new fluorescent nanomaterial with unique properties to replace traditional quantum dots (QDs). The reason is that CDs not only retain the advantages of small size and good luminescence performance of QDs, but also have the advantages of simple preparation, good photostability, good biocompatibility, good water solubility, easy modification and low toxicity [1–3]. Therefore, materials that are based on CDs have become the hot topics in the fields of sensing detection and biological imaging [4–8]. To date, many fluorescent CDs have been developed for the detection of metal ions [9–12], pH [13], flavonoid [14], organic amines [15–17], and biological enzymes [18]. However, the short emission wavelength and low sensitivity of most directly synthesized CDs limit their application in organisms [19].

Anions, such as
HSO
3
-
, play an important role in several key chemical and biological processes [20,21].
HSO
3
-
is a derivative of sulfur dioxide (SO2), which exists in living organisms, also is used in food, beverage, and pharmaceutical production because of its excellent antioxidant, bacterial growth inhibition and bleaching properties, as such, it has attracted the attention of many researchers [22–24]. In addition, SO2 (in the form of
HSO
3
-
) can be produced in vivo, especially in mitochondria, mainly through the oxidation of sulfur-containing amino acids (e.g., cysteine and glutathione) mediated by reactive oxygen species (ROS), which is important for the maintenance of biological sulfur balance [25,26]. In the physiological process,
HSO
3
-
has the functions of lowering blood pressure, inhibiting vascular smooth muscle and regulating heart channels [27,28]. The abnormal concentration of
HSO
3
-
can induce cardiovascular disease, allergic reaction, acute respiratory syndrome, lung cancer and other neurological diseases [29,30]. Therefore, a fast, simple, efficient and accurate method must be established for the real-time detection of endogenous SO2 in organisms. So far, a series of detection techniques, such as spectroscopy, electrochemistry, and luminescence, have been developed to detect the concentration of
HSO
3
-
[31,32]. Among these techniques, fluorescence analysis has become one of the most effective tools for the real-time sensing and visualization of biological species due to its advantages of high sensitivity, high selectivity, high spatial and temporal resolution, low toxicity, and non-invasive detection [33–35]. At present, many fluorescent probes based on different detection mechanisms have been developed for
HSO
3
-
, such as the nucleophilic reaction of aldehydes, Michael-type addition, deprotection of the levulinate and coordination [36]. Nonetheless, most are intensity fluorescent probes, which are susceptible to external factors, such as environmental conditions and abnormal fluctuations in the instrument [37,38]. Ratio fluorescent probes have an advantage over intensity fluorescent probes because they have two non-interfering emission wavelengths [39]. Fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) is recognized as a remarkable method for constructing ratio fluorescence probes. In addition, mitochondria are important organelles in living organisms and are also the main region of
HSO
3
-
production in living cells [40]. As far as we know, ratio fluorescent probes that target the mitochondrial detection of SO2 are few, especially those in situ and in vivo. Therefore, developing a ratio fluorescence probe that targets the mitochondria is of great significance and value to monitor SO2 levels in vitro and in vivo.

In this work, a novel sensing platform was designed and fabricated by utilizing FRET for the rapid and sensitive detection of SO2. The obtained GCDs were employed as energy donors, the specific SO2-sensitive probe (CDDBT) was used as energy acceptors to construct a FRET platform, and the sensing platform displays dual emission bands. This near-infrared emitting ratio fluorescence probe (Mito-GCDs-CDDBT) not only targets the mitochondria but also detects SO2 in cells, zebrafish, and mice successfully. The green-emitting CDs (GCDs) were synthesized by a one-step hydrothermal method using p-amino salicylic acid and o-phenyldiamine as raw materials, and its structure and morphology were characterized. Furthermore, GCDs-CDDBT with high selectivity, high sensitivity, and rapid detection of SO2 was obtained by modifying near-infrared fluorescence emitting molecule (CDDBT) on its surface covalently. Two emission peaks exist, of which the emission peak at 560 nm is attributed to GCDs, and the emission peak at 690 nm is attributed to CDDBT, and an intermolecular FRET also exist. In the presence of
HSO
3
-
, the fluorescence intensity at 690 nm is significantly reduced, whereas that at 560 nm is significantly enhanced because the conjugated structure of CDDBT is destroyed by binding to SO2, and thus the FRET pathway between GCDs and CDDBT is completely inhibited. Cell co-localization experiments showed that GCDs-CDDBT could be targeted well in the mitochondria of cells. More significantly, GCDs-CDDBT can detect SO2 in cells, tissues, zebrafish, and mice. Therefore, the development of the probe has an important guiding significance for monitoring the SO2 levels in organisms and promoting the application of CDs in the field of biosensing.

o-phenylenediamine, p-amino salicylic acid, sodium chloride, disodium hydrogen phosphate, potassium dihydrogen phosphate, 2-methylbenzothiazole, bromopropionic acid, p-diamine phenylacetaldehyde, 4-dimethylamine cinnamaldehyde, and NaHSO3 were purchased from Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). All chemicals and reagents were used directly without further purification. All experiments were carried out using ultrapure water (Heal Force Smart-N ultrapure water machine).

Transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM, Tecnai G2 F20, FEI), atomic force microscope (AFM, Multimode 8, Bruker), UV–Visible spectrophotometer (UH5300, Hitachi), fluorescence spectrophotometer (Cary Eclipse, Varian), Zeta potentiometer (Nano-ZSE, Malvern), X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS, ESCALAB 250Xi, Thermo), enzyme standard instrument (ELx808 BioTeK), confocal laser scanning microscope (FV3000, Olympus), TCSPC system transient fluorescence spectrometer (F900, Edinburgh), research grade inverted fluorescence microscope (Ti2-U, Nikon), and small animal living fluorescence imager (Xtreme, BRUKER).

The specific synthesis of GCDs is detailed as follows: 0.540 g o-phenylenediamine and 0.770 g p-amino salicylic acid were weighed into the beaker, and 25.0 mL ultrapure water was added to stir and dissolve. After completely dissolving, the mixture was transferred into a Teflon reactor and heated to 200 ℃ in an oven for 5 h. Subsequently, the reaction mixture was centrifuged (10000 rpm, 10 min) to remove large solid particles, and the supernatant was filtered through a 0.22 μm microporous membrane. The obtained brown solution underwent dialysis for 24 h (molecular weight cutoff of 1000 Da). After dialysis, the liquid was freeze-dried, and the GCDs powders were obtained.

2-methylbenzothiazole (0.746 g, 5.00 mmol) and 6.00 mL bromopropionate were added in a clean reaction flask that contained 5.00 mL acetonitrile. The mixed solution was refluxed at 90 ℃ for 6 h. After the reaction was completed, the solution was poured into acetonitrile. After centrifuging, the precipitate was added to 300 mL of diethyl ether and centrifuged again to obtain the intermediate. The dried intermediate could be used for subsequent experiments without further treatment. Under the protection of nitrogen, the intermediate (144 mg, 0.510 mmol), 4-dimethylamine cinnamaldehyde (90.0 mg, 0.513 mmol) and piperidine (20.0 µL, 0.200 mmol) were added into a three-necked round-bottomed flask that contained 10.0 mL methanol, and the reaction was continuous refluxed at 70 ℃ for 6 h. Subsequently, the solvent was removed at reduced pressure to obtain the crude product. The crude products were separated and purified by column chromatography (dichloromethane: methanol = 5:1, v/v). The products were collected and concentrated under reduced pressure to obtain blue-purple solid powder 3-(2-carboxyethyl)-2-((1E,3E)-4-(4-(dimethylamino) phenyl) buta-1,3-dien-1-yl) benzo[d]thiazol-3-ium (CDDBT) with a yield of 67.4%. 1H NMR (400 MHz, CD3OD): δ (ppm) 8.31 (t, J = 16.0, 2H), 8.10 (t, 1H), 7.93 (m, 2H), 7.85 (m, 2H), 7.67 (m, 1H), 7.57 (m, 1H), 7.37 (m, 1H), 7.25 (m, 1H), 6.82 (t, J = 16.0, 1H), 5.06 (t, J = 12.0, 2H), 3.71 (s, 2H), 3.08–3.19 (m, 6H). HRMS: m/z calculated for [M]+: 379.1475; found: 379.1480.

A total of 10.0 mg GCDs was dissolved in 10.0 mL ultra-pure water and diluted 100 times to prepare the 0.01 mg/mL GCDs solution. A total of 38.0 mg CDDBT was dissolved in 1.00 mL DMSO and diluted 100 times to prepare CDDBT solution with 1.00 mM mother liquor. The prepared 150 μL GCD solution and 100 μL CDDBT solution were added to the activators EDC and NHS, which were shaken on a shaking table at 25 ℃ for 6 h, respectively. To obtain the final complex GCDs-CDDBT, the reaction solution was centrifuged at 10000 rpm for 15 min, then dialyzed in ultra-pure water for 12 h, and freeze-dried.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selective response of Mito-GCDs-CDDBT to SO2
ce:section.ce:para:
Selective determination: ions (Na+, K+, Cl-, F-, Br-, I-,
HCO
3
-
, NO3 –,
PO
4
3-
,
S 2
O 3
2 -
, HS- and
HSO
3
-
, 80.0 µM), biomolecules (GSH, Cys, Pro, Phe, Met and Tyr, 100 µM) and some ROS (
NO
2
-
, ClO-, ·OH, ONOO–,
O
2
-
, and H2O2, 100 µM) were selected to verify the selectivity of Mito-GCDs-CDDBT. The same amount of different anions or biomolecules and 2.00 mL Mito-GCDs-CDDBT (0.100 mg/mL) solution were added into a fluorescent cuvette. Subsequently, it was measured using a fluorescence spectrometer. A total of 20.0 μL
HSO
3
-
with different concentrations and 2.00 mL Mito-GCDs-CDDBT (0.100 mg/mL) solution were added into a fluorescent cuvette, the final concentrations of
HSO
3
-
were 0, 10, 20, 30, 40, 50, 60, 70, and 80 μM, respectively. Then, the fluorescence emission spectra were determined with Ex = 420 nm (Ex slit width: 10.0 nm, Em slit width: 10.0 nm). The test conditions were 25 ℃, buffer solution PBS (pH 7.40), and reaction time of 1 min.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence imaging of Mito-GCDs-CDDBT in vitro and in vivo
ce:section.ce:para: For cell fluorescence imaging, human non-small cell lung cancer cells (A549) were subcultured on confocal special dishes for 24 h incubation, which were used for cell imaging (ratio fluorescence imaging of exogenous and endogenous SO2) and mitochondrial co-localization experiment. For the cell imaging of endogenous SO2, A549 cells were treated with excess biotin, Na2S2O3 (500 μM), and glutathione (600 μM) for 30 min. Then, Mito-GCDs-CDDBT (0.100 mg/mL) was added for 1 h further incubation. Green channel fluorescence test conditions: λex = 405 nm, λem = 480–600 nm, red channel fluorescence test conditions: λex = 405 nm, λem = 600–720 nm. Mito-Tracker green, a commercial mitochondrial dye, was used for mitochondrial co-localization cell imaging (λex = 488 nm, λem = 500–600 nm).

Tissue sections from SD rat were used for fluorescent tissue imaging. The rats were euthanized, and then their major organs (including heart, liver, spleen, lung and kidney) were removed, embedded in paraffin, and cut into thin slices. The sections were stained for Mito-GCDs-CDDBT (0.100 mg/mL) alone and then with
HSO
3
-
(100 µM), washed with PBS (pH 7.40) for 2–3 times before fluorescence imaging.

All the zebrafish used in the experiment were purchased from the National Zebrafish Resource Center (China) as embryos at the time of purchase and were cultured in the special breeding water for 3–5 days for further experiment. Three groups of experiments were set up, and each group reared 3–5 zebrafish. First, 50.0 μL Mito-GCDs-CDDBT (0.120 mg/mL) was added to the cultured water, incubated for 3 h, and washed with PBS (pH 7.40) for 2–3 times. Then, 20.0 μL different concentrations of
HSO
3
-
(20.0 μM and 100 μM) were added to the second and third groups, respectively. Finally, it was incubated again for 30 min and washed with PBS (pH 7.40) for 2–3 times before fluorescence imaging.

BALB/c nude mice were used for fluorescence imaging in vivo. The nude mice were 5 weeks old and weighed 15.0 g ± 1 g per mouse. All the animals were raised in a sterile environment. The different groups of A549 tumor-bearing nude mice were subcutaneously injected or intratumorally injected for Mito-GCDs-CDDBT (0.120 mg/mL) alone and Mito-GCDs-CDDBT with
HSO
3
-
(100 µM) for 30 min, respectively. All animal experiments were conducted in accordance with the regulations of the Animal Ethics Committee of Shanxi Medical University.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Design of mitochondria-targeted and ratiometric sensing platform for SO2
ce:section.ce:para:
CDDBT, a SO2-specific hemicyanine derivative containing carboxyl group and was also the potential FRET acceptor for GCDs, was synthesized. The synthesized small molecule probe CDDBT had a large conjugate system, which exhibited an obvious fluorescence peak at 690 nm with 550 nm excitation. As nucleophilic ions (for
HSO
3
-
) can attack unsaturated double bond, the conjugated system was destroyed leading to red fluorescence quenching at 690 nm. Encouraged by the good fluorescence property of GCDs, we further used them to construct a sensing platform (GCDs-CDDBT) for the detection of SO2. In this sensing system, the prepared GCDs are unreactive toward SO2, which can emit green fluorescence and serve as an energy donor for FRET system. The CDDBT was synthesized as a responsive moiety for SO2 due to the specific nucleophilic addition. Furthermore, the absorbance of the acceptor CDDBT overlaps well in the wavelength with the emission band of the GCDs, thereby facilitating FRET system formation. The ratiometric fluorescent sensing platform (Mito-GCDs-CDDBT) for SO2, which could target the mitochondria due to its positive charge and achieve in vitro and in vivo biological imaging, was constructed. The detailed research strategy was described and shown in Scheme 1 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication, characterization and optical properties of GCDs
ce:section.ce:para: The GCDs were synthesized using p-amino salicylic acid and o-phenyldiamine as raw materials through one-step hydrothermal method, of which Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave served as a reaction vessel. To obtain the good fluorescence properties of GCDs, the influence of different reaction conditions on fluorescence properties of GCDs was checked. The results were shown in Fig. S1, the optimum reaction condition for GCDs was 200 °C for 5 h, and the two raw materials were mixed in a molar ratio of 1:1.

To confirm the structure of GCDs, the elemental composition and chemical bond were characterized by XPS. As shown in Fig. 1 A, GCDs contained peaks of C1S, O1S, and N1S. The high-resolution C1S spectrum (Fig. S2A) displayed three carbon signals at 288.8, 285.7, and 284.3 eV, which were attributed to the C in the C=O, C-N, and C=C/C-C groups, respectively. The high-resolution N1S spectrum (Fig. S2B) showed three nitrogen signals at 401.8, 400.5, and 398.3 eV, which were attributed to N in the N-O, N-H, and C-N-C groups, respectively. The high-resolution O1S XPS spectrum (Fig. S2C) showed three oxygen signals at 533.6, 532.8, and 530.7 eV, which represented the O in C-O, C-OH/C-O-C, and C=O groups, respectively. XPS results showed that the prepared GCDs were rich in carboxyl, amino, and hydroxyl groups.

The morphology and size of GCDs were characterized. As seen in Fig. 1B, GCDs were spherical in morphology with good dispersion and no obvious agglomeration by TEM. The diameter of GCDs was 1–5 nm, and the average size was 2.78 nm (Fig. 1C). GCDs also displayed good dispersion in water as validated by AFM (Fig. 1D). As shown in Fig. 1E, the thickness of GCDs was mainly concentrated at 1–4 nm, which was in good agreement with the TEM results.

To further confirm the functional groups on the surface of GCDs, Fourier transform-infrared (FT-IR) analysis was adopted. As depicted in Fig. 1F, a relatively wide absorption shoulder band occurred at the wavenumber of 3415 cm−1, which belonged to O-H or N-H stretching vibration. Bands appeared at 2925 cm−1, 1625 cm−1 and 1509 cm−1 were attributed to the stretching vibration of C-H, C=O, and C=C, respectively. The bands at 1388 cm−1 and 1138 cm−1 were ascribed to the bending vibration of NH2 and C-O, respectively. The above results indicated that GCDs contained carboxyl group, amino group, and other groups on the surface, which were consistent with those of XPS. The crystal composition of GCDs was further characterized by XRD in Fig. 1G, and the diffraction peak near 2θ = 25° was strong, thereby suggesting that the synthesized GCDs were mainly composed of amorphous carbon [41]. The above results indicated that GCDs were endowed with groups to be easily functionalized, thereby ensuring the potential application of GCDs, such as a carrier.

Next, the optical properties of GCDs were assessed with fluorescence and UV–Vis spectrophotometer. As seen in Fig. 1H, the UV–Vis absorption spectra of the GCDs showed strong absorption at 420 nm, which were derived from the n-π* transition of C=O bonds of oxygen-containing functional groups [42]. The optimal fluorescence emission spectra at 560 nm could be observed under excitation at 420 nm. The fluorescence emission spectra were measured at different excitation wavelengths that range from 400 nm to 480 nm. Fig. 1I showed that the fluorescence peak at 560 nm remained unchanged, indicating that the GCDs had an excitation-independent fluorescence behavior. Next, the potential effects of ion concentration, xenon lamp exposure, and pH on stability property of GCDs were analyzed. The ions and xenon lamp irradiation did not induce obvious alteration of the fluorescence intensity of GCDs (Figs. S3A, B). In Fig. S3C, the fluorescence intensity of GCDs displayed a bell-shaped curve when the pH increased from 4.00 to 10.0, which might be caused by the protonation and deprotonation of amino groups or carboxyl groups on the GCDs surface. The fluorescence of GCDs reached the maximum and remained stable at pH 7.40. The above results suggested that GCDs had good stability in physiological condition and could be potentially used in biomedical research.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of FRET nano-probe (Mito-GCDs-CDDBT)
ce:section.ce:para: To prepare FRET nano-probe, a SO2 responsive near-infrared emission organic molecule (CDDBT), was designed and synthesized. The detailed synthetic route was shown in Scheme S1. CDDBT was characterized by 1H NMR spectrum and HRMS spectrum (Figs. S4 and S5). Then, CDDBT was assembled onto the GCDs via the amidation reaction between the amino moieties of GCDs and the carboxyl groups of the CDDBT to obtain FRET nano-probe Mito-GCDs-CDDBT. As seen in Fig. 2 A, the morphology of Mito-GCDs-CDDBT was characterized by HRTEM, the diameter of the assembly was approximately 4.95 nm, which was larger than that of GCDs (2.78 nm), and no aggregation occurred. Furthermore, the conjugation of the GCDs with CDDBT was validated by FT-IR spectroscopy. As shown in Fig. S6, the characteristic bands appeared at 1569 cm−1 and 1705 cm−1, which were attributed to the benzene stretching modes of CDDBT and the C=O from amide groups, indicating the covalent conjugation of CDDBT with the GCDs. In addition, zeta potential results showed that GCDs was − 9.2 mV, and conjugation of CDDBT with GCDs led to an increase of the zeta potential from − 9.2 mV to 12.5 mV (Fig. 2B).

To verify FRET occurred between GCDs and CDDBT, the nano assembly was characterized by the UV–Vis absorption spectrum and fluorescence emission spectrum (Fig. 2C). The absorption peaks of GCDs and CDDBT were located at 420 nm and 525 nm, and the fluorescence emission peaks of GCDs and CDDBT were located at 560 nm and 690 nm, respectively. The absorption band of CDDBT well overlapped with that of GCDs. As shown in Fig. 2D, the nano-probe Mito-GCDs-CDDBT displayed a wide absorption range of 400–700 nm, including GCDs and CDDBT absorption peaks. Meanwhile, with excitation of 420 nm, the fluorescence emission spectrum of the nano-probe showed two fluorescence emission peaks, with a strong fluorescence peak at 690 nm and a weak peak at 560 nm, suggesting that FRET indeed occurred between GCDs (donor) and the CDDBT (acceptor). As shown in Fig.S7, with Xenon lamp excitation for 10 min, the fluorescence of nano assembly at 560 nm and 690 nm remained stable, indicating the good photostability of Mito-GCDs-CDDBT.

The selectivity of Mito-GCDs-CDDBT toward SO2 was first examined. As shown in Fig. 3 A, among the various analytes (e.g., Na+, K+, Cl-, F-, Br-, I-,
HCO
3
-
,
NO
3
-
,
PO
4
3-
,
S
2
O
3
2-
, HS-, GSH, Cys, Pro, Phe, Met, Tyr, and
HSO
3
-
) and other ROS including
NO
2
-
, ClO-, ·OH, ONOO–,
O
2
-
, and H2O2, only
HSO
3
-
induced the fluorescence intensity ratio value (F560/F690) augmentation of Mito-GCDs-CDDBT, which indicated its excellent selectivity and facilitated its application. To examine the dose-dependent response of Mito-GCDs-CDDBT to
HSO
3
-
, different amounts of
HSO
3
-
were added to the nano assembly. As shown in Fig. 3B, detailed photoluminescence titration was performed at 420 nm excitation wavelength, with the increase of concentration of
HSO
3
-
, the intensity of emission band at 690 nm decreased while the intensity of the emission band at 560 nm increased, thereby suggesting that
HSO
3
-
reacted with the nano-probe and disrupted the FRET between GCDs and CDDBT. In Fig. 3C, the ratio of the fluorescence intensity values of two spectral bands (F560/F690) of Mito-GCDs-CDDBT, which quantitatively changed with a good linear relationship with the NaHSO3 concentrations between 0 and 80.0 µΜ (R2 = 0.998), were further analyzed. The detection limit of Mito-GCDs-CDDBT for
HSO
3
-
was calculated to be 0.701 µM. Compared with other reported SO2 probes, Mito-GCDs-CDDBT exhibited superior sensitivity (Table S1).

The fluorescence response stability of Mito-GCDs-CDDBT toward
HSO
3
-
was studied under different conditions. As depicted in Fig. S8A, the ratiometric fluorescence response to
HSO
3
-
was stable in physiological conditions, as well as in the presence of NaCl solution (300 μM). To apply the Mito-GCDs-CDDBT to biological systems, the optimal response to pH was also checked. The fluorescence intensity ratio of Mito-GCDs-CDDBT did not change significantly in the pH range from 4.00 to 10.0 (Fig. S8B), indicating it had good stability over a wide pH range. However, after adding
HSO
3
-
, the fluorescence intensity ratio exhibited a noticeable increment at pH 7.40. These results proved the high sensitivity and selectivity of Mito-GCDs-CDDBT to
HSO
3
-
under physiological. In addition, the response time of Mito-GCDs-CDDBT toward
HSO
3
-
was investigated. As shown in Fig. S8C, when different amounts of
HSO
3
-
was added, the response was all completed within 30 s, indicating its fast response ability to
HSO
3
-
.

When donor participated in the energy transfer process, the fluorescence lifetime of the donor would decrease [43]. Therefore, fluorescence lifetime could serve as the proof for excited state energy transfer process. As seen in Fig. 3D, the time resolved fluorescence of GCDs and GCDs-CDDBT was measured, which clearly presented the existence of the FRET process between the prepared GCDs and CDDBT. The time-resolved fluorescence measurement of the GCDs alone was doubling exponential with an average lifetime of 3.34 ns. Moreover, the average fluorescence lifetime of GCDs-CDDBT solution was 2.59 ns. The reduction of lifetime of GCDs after coating with CDDBT supported the presence of an excited state energy transfer process in nano-probe. According to the FRET principle, the donor (GCDs) first absorbed the energy of the incident light and then directly transfered the excited state energy to the nearby receptor (CDDBT) molecule without emitting fluorescence. This process was destroyed with the addition of SO2, because it can react with CDDBT. The detailed process was shown in Fig. 3E. Next, the absorption spectrum of Mito-GCDs-CDDBT with the increase in the concentration of
HSO
3
-
was studied. As shown in Fig. S9, the absorption peak at 525 nm gradually decreased due to the reaction of
HSO
3
-
with CDDBT.

Before its bio-application, the cytotoxicity of Mito-GCDs-CDDBT was assessed by MTT assay. A549 cells were selected as the imaging model. Moreover, A549 cells incubated with GCDs (up to 150 μg/mL) did not show obvious cell death, and the cell viability of A549 cells subjected to Mito-GCDs-CDDBT as high as 140 μg/mL was above 85% in Fig. S10. Mito-GCDs-CDDBT had neglectable cytotoxicity and was applicable for
HSO
3
-
detection in organisms.

After confirming the biocompatibility of Mito-GCDs-CDDBT, its ability to sense exogenous
HSO
3
-
was studied. As shown in Fig. 4 A, A549 cells subjected to Mito-GCDs-CDDBT showed weak green fluorescence and strong red fluorescence signal due to the intramolecular FRET. With the addition of
HSO
3
-
, the green fluorescence signal gradually increased, and the red fluorescence signal decreased, indicating the disruption of FRET by
HSO
3
-
. Subsequently, the ability of Mito-GCDs-CDDBT detecting endogenous
HSO
3
-
was further investigated. GSH and Na2S2O3 were reported to induce endogenous SO2 generation in living cells [22]. Herein, A549 cells were treated with Na2S2O3 and GSH for 1 h and then Mito-GCDs-CDDBT were added. As shown in Fig. 4A, B, green fluorescence signal increased while the red one decreased, indicating that Mito-GCDs-CDDBT could detect endogenous
HSO
3
-
at the cellular level. Those results indicated that Mito-GCDs-CDDBT could sense exogenous and endogenous
HSO
3
-
. To confirm the cellular localization of Mito-GCDs-CDDBT, co-stained experiments with commercially available MitoTracker Green were performed. As shown in Fig. 4C, signal derived from Mito-GCDs-CDDBT (red) colocalized well with that of MitoTracker dye (green) with a high Pearson’s coefficient of 0.92 due to its positive charge. Moreover, the fluorescence intensity distribution curves of red and green channels were closely synchronized in linear areas of interest. These results confirmed that Mito-GCDs-CDDBT could anchor in the mitochondria and monitor SO2 in the mitochondria of cell.

After confirming the tissue
HSO
3
-
imaging ability of Mito-GCDs-CDDBT, we further investigated its application at tissue level. Sections of major organs of mice were incubated with Mito-GCDs-CDDBT in the presence and absence of
HSO
3
-
. As shown in Fig. 5 A-E, with the presence of
HSO
3
-
, tissues showed stronger green fluorescence signal, whereas tissues showed stronger red fluorescence signal in absence of
HSO
3
-
. The quantification of fluorescence intensity also confirmed above observation (Fig. 5F). These results suggested that that the synthesized nano-probe could be used for the detection of tissues
HSO
3
-
.

These cellular and tissue imaging results inspired us to further explore the possible applications of Mito-GCDs-CDDBT for SO2 detection in vivo. As seen in Fig. 6 , zebrafish incubated with Mito-GCDs-CDDBT (0.120 mg/mL) emitted strong red fluorescence and weak green fluorescence signal. However, after adding
HSO
3
-
(100 µM), red fluorescence signal weakened, and green one enhanced, mainly due to the disruption of FRET by
HSO
3
-
. Therefore, the above results implied that robust nanocomplex probes could detect SO2 in zebrafish.

BALB/c nude mice were recruited to validate the application of Mito-GCDs-CDDBT monitoring SO2 in mammals. Mice were subjected to Mito-GCDs-CDDBT (0.120 mg/mL) alone or Mito-GCDs-CDDBT with
HSO
3
-
(100 µM) subcutaneous or intratumoral injection. As shown in Fig. 7 , in Mito-GCDs-CDDBT alone treated mice, both subcutaneous or intratumoral injection displayed strong red and weak green fluorescence signal, whereas in Mito-GCDs-CDDBT together with
HSO
3
-
mice showed strong green and weak red fluorescence signal due to the disruption of FRET. Combined with those results in vitro, all of them suggested that Mito-GCDs-CDDBT could successfully detect SO2 in vitro and in vivo.

In summary, we designed and synthesized a novel FRET nanoplatform called Mito-GCDs-CDDBT for the rapid and specific detection of SO2 in vitro and in vivo. The GCDs served as energy donors, and SO2-specific probes (CDDBT) served as an energy acceptor that displayed dual emission bands. Mito-GCDs-CDDBT exhibited high efficiency and selectivity, good stability, and quick response ability in SO2 sensing. Furthermore, Mito-GCDs-CDDBT achieved the ratiometric imaging of exogenous and endogenous SO2 in living cells and showed excellent mitochondrial targeting ability. Notably, Mito-GCDs-CDDBT realized the specific SO2 detection tissues of different mouse organs and living animals. Present study paved ways for the fabrication of nanoplatform for SO2 sensing, which held great potential for the theranostics of SO2-involved diseases.

Wen Liu: Investigation, Methodology, Data curation, Funding acquisition. Jinyao Li: Data curation. Zicheng Wang: Resources, Investigation, Data curation. Yafei Tian: . Guodong Ren: Methodology. Xiaoyu Hou: Resources, Investigation. Lixia Guo: Data curation. Lihong Li: Data curation, Funding acquisition. Chengwu Zhang: Supervision. Zhifang Wu: Data curation, Supervision. Lili Yan: Investigation, Methodology, Funding acquisition. Sijin Li: Conceptualization, Writing – review & editing. Haipeng Diao: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition.

J. Lumin.
